---
title: "3.3 Virtual Pet and Community Class"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Groupwork Coding Challenge {.image-fit}

### Virtual Pet Class

![Virtual Pet](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-3-anatomy-of-a-class/virtualpet.jpg){fig-align="center" width="28%"}

In the late 1990s and early 2000s, digital pets like in the photo were a huge hit.

You had to feed, play, and interact with your virtual pet in order to keep it healthy and happy.

Let's think about how they were programmed.

## Virtual Pet Design

### Step 1: Object-oriented design

In pairs, brainstorm about the **object-oriented design** for the virtual pet.

What data or instance variables would you need to keep track of for a virtual pet?

What behaviors or methods would the virtual pet have?

You could draw a class diagram, although it is not required.

## Virtual Pet Requirements

### Instance variables

Declare at least three instance variables in the `VirtualPet` class.

Two of the instance variables should be called `name` and `health`.

`health` is a number from 0 to 10.

The rest can be variables from your design.

Do not forget their `private` access modifiers.

## Virtual Pet Methods

### Print, feed, and one more behavior

Complete the `print` method to print out the instance variables of the `VirtualPet` object.

Complete the `feed` method to add `1` to the `health` instance variable.

Create at least one more method that changes one of the instance variables from your design.

## Virtual Pet Main

### Construct and call methods

Complete the `main` method to construct at least two `VirtualPet` objects.

Use the `VirtualPet` constructor given to you with arguments for `name` and `health`.

Then use at least one of the objects to call its `feed` and `print` methods.

## Active Code

### `activecode:: challenge-VirtualPet-Class`

Complete the `VirtualPet` class below.

Add at least three instance variables, a `print` method, a `feed` method, and at least one other method that changes an instance variable.

Then complete the `main` method to construct at least two `VirtualPet` objects and call the `feed` and `print` methods with one of them.

## VirtualPet Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class VirtualPet
{
    // write 3 instance variables for VirtualPet's name,
    // health (as a number), and at least 1 other from your design
    // Remember the pattern:  private type variableName;



    // constructor written for you - do not change
    public VirtualPet(String initName, int initHealth)
    {
        // the constructor sets the instance variables to the init parameter variables
        name = initName;
        health = initHealth;
    }

    // Print the VirtualPet's data (instance variables) with System.out.println
    public void print()
    {
        // print the instance variables with spaces between them

    }

    // Complete the feed method below to add to the health instance variable
    public void feed()
    {
        // add 1 to the health variable

    }

    // Create another public void method that changes one of the instance variables


    // get method used for testing - do not change
    public int getHealth()
    {
        return health;
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        VirtualPet p = new VirtualPet("Fluffy", 5);
        // call the VirtualPet constructor to create another new object with a different name and health

        // call the pet object p's feed() method

        // call the other method that you created

        // call the pet object p's print() method

    }
}
```
:::

## VirtualPet Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The Runestone tests check:

::: {.tight-list}
- at least three `public void` methods
- `print()` output contains `Fluffy`
- `feed()` changes health from `5` to `6`
- `feed()` changes health from `1` to `2`
- another `VirtualPet` object is constructed
- instance variable types include `String int`
- at least three private instance variables
:::

## Design a Class for Your Community

### Continuing unit project

In this unit, you will design a class of your own choice that is relevant to your community.

You will improve the class in each lesson of this unit.

The textbook suggests that you work in pairs on this project.

## Community Brainstorm

### Step 1

Brainstorm and ask people in your community what would be important or useful for them to track in a community organization or activity.

Examples include:

::: {.tight-list}
- community events
- club activities
- athletic games or statistics
- community leaders
- performers or performances
- health tracking
:::

## Community Class Design

### Step 2

Come up with the class name for your community data-tracking class.

It should be a category of people or things, not a specific person or thing.

Come up with at least three instance variables that are attributes of things in that class.

Think about what data type each variable should be.

## Community Active Code

### `activecode:: community-challenge1`

Type in your class name and declare the instance variables for your class in the active code exercise.

Click on run.

Your class will not print out anything yet, but it should run without errors.

You will add constructors and other methods to this class in the next lessons.

## Community Starter

```java
public class          // Add your class name here!
{
    // write 3 instance variables for class: private type variableName;



    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
      // Run your code to check for errors.
      // It will not print anything yet.
      // You will add more in the next lesson.
    }
}
```

## Community Test Target

### Runestone private-variable check

The Runestone test checks for at least three private instance variables.

```text
Expected: 3 Private
Check: Checking private instance variable(s)
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- list at least three `VirtualPet` instance variables, including `name` and `health`
- keep `name` and `health` as private instance variables
- complete `print`, `feed`, and one additional mutating method
- construct at least two `VirtualPet` objects in `main`
- design a community class name as a category, not a specific item
- declare at least three private instance variables for the community class
:::

## End

### 3.3 complete

Next topic: 3.4 Constructors.
